perturbation.original_text: Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing shall not apply with respect to any information after five years following the disclosure thereof or any inform ation that the Receiving Party can document (i) is or becomes (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any affiliate, agent, consultant or employee) generally available to the public, or (ii) was in its possession or known by it prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party as evidenced in writing, except to the extent that such information was unlawfully appropriated, or (iii) was rightfully disclosed to it by a third party, or (iv) was independently developed without use of any Propr ietary Information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing shall not apply with respect to any information after ninety days following the disclosure thereof or any inform ation that the Receiving Party can document (i) is or becomes (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any affiliate, agent, consultant or employee) generally available to the public, or (ii) was in its possession or known by it prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party as evidenced in writing, except to the extent that such information was unlawfully appropriated, or (iii) was rightfully disclosed to it by a third party, or (iv) was independently developed without use of any Propr ietary Information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 90 days may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with state trade secret laws which often imply a longer, more reasonable duration for protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act states that misappropriation of a trade secret can occur if the secret is disclosed or used without consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. A 90-day protection period may not be considered sufficient to prevent such misappropriation, particularly if the information has a longer commercial lifespan, potentially conflicting with the intent to provide reasonable protection to trade secret holders.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the confidentiality period from five years to ninety days. The Law Explanation suggests this may not provide sufficient protection for trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the intent to provide reasonable protection. The Perturbation Explanation also states the reduced period may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient to protect trade secrets, conflicting with state trade secret laws. Although the law snippets are missing, the explanations provided strongly suggest that a 90-day protection period is unreasonably short and thus contradicts the intent of trade secret laws, which aim to offer meaningful protection.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may make disclosures required by law or court order provided the Receiving Party uses diligent reasonable efforts to limit disclosure and has allowed the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may make disclosures required by law or court order without notifying the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for 'diligent reasonable efforts to limit disclosure' and notifying the Disclosing Party violates the spirit of confidentiality agreements and could conflict with legal duties to protect sensitive information, as it potentially disregards the Disclosing Party's legal standing to intervene.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c) allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information. By removing the requirement that the Receiving Party allow the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order, the modified text prevents the Disclosing Party from exercising their right under Rule 26(c) to protect their confidential information in legal proceedings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Receiving Party to allow the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order, which contradicts the spirit of Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c). The Law Explanation states that this rule allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information. By removing the requirement that the Receiving Party allow the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order, the modified text prevents the Disclosing Party from exercising their right under Rule 26(c) to protect their confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Rule 26 and its provisions for governing discovery. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that the modification potentially disregards the Disclosing Party's legal standing to intervene.

perturbation.original_text: Immediately upon the written request by the Disclosing Party at any time, the Receiving Party will return to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copi es or extracts thereof, save that where such Proprietary Information is a form incapable of return or has been copied or transcribed into another document, it shall be destroyed or erased, as appropriate.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will return to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information within 90 days of the request. The Receiving Party is not required to destroy or erase Proprietary Information that has been copied or transcribed into another document.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of proprietary information and eliminating the obligation to destroy or erase copied information conflicts with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts, as it allows the Receiving Party to retain and potentially misuse confidential information for an extended period.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 imposes a duty of good faith and fair dealing in the performance and enforcement of a contract. Allowing the Receiving Party to delay the return of confidential information for 90 days and retain copies goes against the spirit of the NDA, potentially violating the duty of good faith by unreasonably delaying the return of confidential data and permitting retention of the data. This introduces uncertainty and increases the risk of misuse.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the duty of good faith and fair dealing. The original contract requires the Receiving Party to return or destroy Proprietary Information immediately upon request. The changed text allows the Receiving Party to delay the return for 90 days and removes the obligation to destroy or erase copied information. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation highlight that this delay and retention of copies goes against the spirit of the NDA and increases the risk of misuse. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that good faith includes 'faithful performance of duties' and 'observance of fair dealing standards,' implying the Receiving Party should promptly return confidential information and prevent its misuse, as specified in the original agreement. The modified text undermines these duties, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the jurisdiction in which the Disclosing Party is located (or if the Disclosing Party is based in more than one country, the country in which its headquart ers are located) (the "Territory") and the parties agree to submit disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement to the non -exclusive of the courts in the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Delaware, regardless of the location of the Disclosing Party. All disputes will be resolved through binding arbitration in a location of the Receiving Party's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing arbitration and specifying Delaware law, regardless of the Disclosing Party's location, can be problematic if the Disclosing Party is located in a jurisdiction with stronger trade secret protections or different legal standards, thereby potentially weakening the protections afforded to the Disclosing Party and conflicting with laws related to jurisdiction and venue.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a) allows a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. By mandating binding arbitration in a location chosen by the Receiving Party, the modified text potentially contradicts this law by limiting the Disclosing Party's ability to seek a more convenient and just venue for resolving disputes, especially if the chosen location is distant or unfavorable to the Disclosing Party.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates binding arbitration in a location chosen by the Receiving Party and specifies Delaware law, regardless of the Disclosing Party's location. This potentially contradicts 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a), as described in both law snippets, which allows a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. By limiting the Disclosing Party's ability to seek a more convenient venue, especially if the chosen location is distant or unfavorable, the modified text directly restricts access to a potentially more suitable legal venue. The original text allowed for disputes to be submitted to the courts in the Disclosing Party's location. The new text removes this option. This constitutes a legally significant contradiction because it removes a possible venue for resolving disputes.
